title: The old keyword
checkLabels.t7: There are two names, not one
why.untouched: script.js is exactly as it was.
hint2.untouched: Look at the two numbers in the console. One of them should not be what it is.
why.not-closed: A tag in index.html is open and never closed.
why.not-linked: The script never reaches the page.
why.crashed: Your code stopped with an error.
hint2.crashed: Look at the Console panel below.
hint3.crashed: Declare the same name twice with let at the same level and the code breaks. That is exactly what var allowed silently.
why.var-left: There is still a var somewhere.
hint2.var-left: All three declarations change, not just the problematic one.
why.outer-not-let: Not both discounts are declared with let.
hint2.outer-not-let: Both the outer and the inner one are declared with let. They are two separate names.
why.wrong-shape: The name discount does not appear four times. The task is to keep two declarations and two prints.
hint2.wrong-shape: Do not remove the block and do not remove the second declaration. The point is for both names to exist at once without interfering.
why.leaked: The outer discount is no longer 0. The inner one has leaked out.
hint2.leaked: let stops at the edge of a block. var does not.
hint3.leaked: With var, the same name is declared a second time and simply overwrites the first. With let, a SEPARATE name is created inside the block, and it lives only there.
hint4.leaked: Change both declarations to let. The inner one becomes its own name and the outer one stays 0.
why.no-sold: There is no name sold holding 1247.
why.wrong-count: There are not two lines in the console.
why.lost-p: A line has disappeared from the page.
blocks.0.text: Look at script.js. One name, two prints. Between them there is a block — the two curly braces — that sets discount to 50.
blocks.1.text: The console shows 0 and then 50. That looks reasonable. And that is the trap: the block was never supposed to change anything outside itself.
blocks.3.text: let and const stop at the edge of a block. Whatever is declared inside lives only inside. var does not work that way — it leaks out to the nearest function, or to the whole file.
blocks.4.text: So the second var discount does not create a new name. It finds the old one and overwrites it. One name, two different lives, zero warnings.
blocks.6.text: You will meet the claim that var is banned or removed. It is not. It works in every browser, it will work in twenty years, and millions of lines of existing code use it.
blocks.7.text: var has DIFFERENT rules. Older ones, from a time when the language had no block-level names at all. let and const arrived in 2015 precisely to fix that.
blocks.8.text: So you will see it in other people's code and in old tutorials. You need to know what it does — not to think of it as broken.
blocks.10.text: var lets the same name be declared twice. let does not — it breaks on the spot with a SyntaxError.
blocks.11.text: That sounds like a restriction. It is really a warning: if you are declaring the same name twice, one of them is almost certainly a typo or forgotten code.
blocks.12.text: There is a third thing — var behaves strangely before the line it is declared on. But that needs functions, and you will meet it in section five.
blocks.14.text: Change all three var to let. Do not remove the block and do not remove the second declaration.
blocks.15.text: After that the console should show 0 and then 0 again. The inner discount will exist, but only inside the block.
